description: UE5 networking model: property replication, RPCs, network roles, ownership, relevancy, and multiplayer architecture

entries.title: Replication Overview
entries.description: UE5 uses a server-authoritative model. The server runs the game simulation and replicates state to clients. Clients send input to the server via RPCs. The server validates and applies changes, which then replicate back.
entries.whatItIs: UE5's replication system is a server-authoritative networking framework where the server maintains the canonical game state and pushes property changes to connected clients, while clients communicate intentions to the server through Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs).
whenToUse: When building any multiplayer game that requires synchronized state between players
When game logic must be validated by a central authority to prevent cheating
When you need actors, properties, or events to be visible across multiple connected machines
For any online or LAN multiplayer mode including co-op, competitive, or MMO-style games
whenNotToUse: For purely single-player games with no networking requirements
When a peer-to-peer lockstep model is more appropriate (e.g., deterministic RTS games)
For cosmetic-only effects that have no gameplay impact — handle those locally instead
When you need extremely low-latency input mirroring and server authority adds unacceptable delay
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set bReplicates = true on the actor, causing no replication to occur at all
Trying to replicate properties from client to server — property replication is server-to-client only
Spawning replicated actors on clients instead of on the server, causing desync
Setting Net Update Frequency too high, wasting bandwidth, or too low, causing stale data on clients
Not understanding that the server is the only machine that should make authoritative gameplay decisions
keyPoints: Server-authoritative: the server is the source of truth for all game state
Property replication: server → clients (one-way). Clients cannot replicate to server
RPCs: function calls that execute on a different machine (Server, Client, or NetMulticast)
The actor must have bReplicates = true to participate in networking
Only the server should spawn replicated actors — they auto-appear on clients
Net Update Frequency controls how often an actor checks for replicated property changes

entries.title: Property Replication Setup
entries.description: To replicate a property from server to clients, mark it with UPROPERTY(Replicated) or UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Func), and register it in GetLifetimeReplicatedProps().
entries.whatItIs: Property replication is UE5's mechanism for automatically synchronizing UPROPERTY-marked variables from the server to clients, with optional OnRep callback functions that fire on the client when the value changes, enabling reactive UI updates and gameplay responses.
whenToUse: When a gameplay variable (health, ammo, score, team) must be visible to one or more clients
When clients need to react to value changes via OnRep callbacks (e.g., updating a health bar)
When using COND_OwnerOnly for private player data like inventory or ability cooldowns
When using COND_InitialOnly for data that only needs to be sent once (team color, player name)
whenNotToUse: For cosmetic or local-only data that does not affect gameplay across machines
When the data changes extremely frequently and bandwidth is a concern — consider RPCs or custom serialization
For transient effects like particle spawn locations that are better sent via unreliable Multicast RPCs
When the property is only needed on the server (e.g., internal AI state)
commonMistakes: Declaring UPROPERTY(Replicated) but forgetting to register it in GetLifetimeReplicatedProps() — replication silently fails
Forgetting to call Super::GetLifetimeReplicatedProps() in the override, breaking parent class replication
Expecting OnRep functions to fire on the server — they only fire on clients receiving the replicated value
Using ReplicatedUsing but misspelling the OnRep function name, causing a compile or link error
Not understanding replication conditions — sending data to all clients when COND_OwnerOnly would reduce bandwidth
keyPoints: Step 1: UPROPERTY(Replicated) or UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_FuncName)
Step 2: override GetLifetimeReplicatedProps() and add DOREPLIFETIME(Class, Property)
Both steps are required — missing either one means no replication
OnRep functions fire on clients when the replicated value changes
DOREPLIFETIME_CONDITION for conditional replication: COND_OwnerOnly, COND_SkipOwner, COND_InitialOnly, COND_Custom
COND_InitialOnly: replicates once then never again (good for static data like team color)

entries.title: RPCs (Remote Procedure Calls)
entries.description: RPCs are functions that execute on a different machine. Server RPCs are called by the owning client and run on the server. Client RPCs are called by the server and run on the owning client. Multicast RPCs run on server and all clients.
entries.whatItIs: RPCs (Remote Procedure Calls) are UFUNCTION-marked functions that, when called on one machine, execute on a different machine — enabling clients to request actions from the server (Server RPCs), the server to notify specific clients (Client RPCs), or the server to broadcast events to all connected machines (NetMulticast RPCs).
whenToUse: Server RPCs: when a client needs to request an action from the server (fire weapon, use ability, interact)
Client RPCs: when the server needs to notify a specific client of something (show UI message, play local effect)
NetMulticast RPCs: when all clients need to see the same event (explosion effect, global announcement)
Use Reliable for gameplay-critical events that must not be lost (damage, ability activation, state changes)
Use Unreliable for cosmetic effects that can be skipped under network congestion (particles, sounds)
whenNotToUse: Do not use RPCs for continuously changing state — use property replication instead (e.g., health, position)
Do not use Reliable RPCs for high-frequency cosmetic events — this can saturate the reliable buffer and crash
Avoid Server RPCs from actors the client does not own — they will be rejected by the engine
Do not use Multicast for data only one client needs — use Client RPCs to save bandwidth
commonMistakes: Calling a Server RPC from an actor the client does not own — the call is silently dropped
Using Reliable for high-frequency events (like every-frame updates), overflowing the reliable buffer and causing disconnects
Forgetting to implement the _Implementation() function — the RPC compiles but does nothing
Not adding WithValidation on Server RPCs, missing an opportunity to reject malicious or invalid input
Calling a NetMulticast RPC from a client instead of the server — it will only execute locally
keyPoints: Server RPC: UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable). Client calls → executes on server. Must be called by the owning connection.
Client RPC: UFUNCTION(Client, Reliable). Server calls → executes on owning client only.
NetMulticast RPC: UFUNCTION(NetMulticast, Reliable). Server calls → executes on server AND all clients.
Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered. Use for important events (damage, ability activation).
Unreliable: may be dropped under congestion. Use for cosmetic/non-critical (animation cues, particles).
WithValidation: adds a _Validate function for anti-cheat checking on Server RPCs.
Implementation goes in FuncName_Implementation(). Validation in FuncName_Validate().

entries.title: Network Roles & Ownership
entries.description: Every replicated actor has a Local Role and Remote Role. Understanding these is essential for writing correct multiplayer code.
entries.whatItIs: Network Roles (Authority, AutonomousProxy, SimulatedProxy, None) define what each machine's relationship is to a given actor, while Ownership tracks which client connection controls a given actor — together they determine what code runs where and who can call Server RPCs.
whenToUse: Check HasAuthority() to gate server-only logic like applying damage or spawning projectiles
Check IsLocallyControlled() to gate client-only logic like input handling or local camera effects
Use role checks to run different logic paths on server vs. owning client vs. other clients
Verify ownership when debugging Server RPCs that are silently failing
whenNotToUse: Do not rely solely on role checks for security — always validate on the server side
Avoid complex role-checking logic when simpler HasAuthority() or IsLocallyControlled() suffice
Do not assume ROLE_Authority always means dedicated server — in listen server, the host is both authority and a client
commonMistakes: Confusing HasAuthority() with IsLocallyControlled() — they answer different questions
Forgetting that on a listen server, the host's Pawn is both Authority AND locally controlled
Not setting ownership correctly, causing Server RPCs to be rejected by the engine
Running cosmetic effects on the server (where no one sees them) instead of checking for local role
Assuming ROLE_SimulatedProxy actors receive OnRep callbacks — they do, but at the replication rate, not immediately
keyPoints: ROLE_Authority: this machine owns the authoritative simulation (usually the server)
ROLE_AutonomousProxy: the owning client's version of their own Pawn. Has input prediction.
ROLE_SimulatedProxy: other clients' view of someone else's Pawn. Receives replicated state only.
ROLE_None: not replicated
HasAuthority(): returns true if this machine has ROLE_Authority for this actor
IsLocallyControlled(): true on the machine that owns input for this Pawn
Ownership chain: Connection → PlayerController → Pawn. Server RPCs require correct ownership.

entries.title: Net Relevancy & Priority
entries.description: Not all actors replicate to all clients at all times. Net Relevancy determines which clients receive updates for which actors.
entries.whatItIs: Net Relevancy is UE5's system for filtering which actors replicate to which clients based on distance, ownership, and custom logic, combined with Net Priority which determines bandwidth allocation order when the network is saturated — together they optimize multiplayer bandwidth usage.
whenToUse: Use NetCullDistanceSquared to stop replicating actors too far from a client's viewpoint
Use bAlwaysRelevant for globally important actors like GameState or match timers
Use bOnlyRelevantToOwner for private player data like inventory or personal HUD state
Use Dormancy for actors that rarely change to reduce unnecessary replication checks
Override IsNetRelevantFor() when you need custom relevancy logic beyond simple distance
whenNotToUse: Do not set bAlwaysRelevant on every actor — this defeats the purpose of relevancy culling
Avoid extremely small NetCullDistanceSquared values that cause actors to pop in/out noticeably
Do not rely on relevancy alone for security — clients may have seen the data before the actor became irrelevant
commonMistakes: Setting bAlwaysRelevant on too many actors, causing unnecessary bandwidth usage
Forgetting that NetCullDistanceSquared is squared — passing a linear distance value results in an extremely small cull range
Not using Dormancy for mostly-static actors, wasting CPU cycles checking for changes every net update
Assuming actors become irrelevant instantly — there is hysteresis to prevent rapid load/unload flickering
Not considering that when an actor becomes relevant again, all its replicated properties are re-sent (initial burst)
keyPoints: NetCullDistanceSquared: actors farther than this from the client's viewpoint are not relevant
bAlwaysRelevant: if true, this actor replicates to ALL clients regardless of distance
bOnlyRelevantToOwner: if true, only replicates to the owning client (good for inventory, private data)
IsNetRelevantFor(): virtual function you can override for custom relevancy logic
NetPriority: higher priority actors get bandwidth first when the network is saturated
Dormancy: actors can go dormant to stop checking for replication (wake on property change)

entries.title: Character Movement Replication & Prediction
entries.description: CharacterMovementComponent has built-in client-side prediction. The client predicts movement locally, sends input to the server, and the server corrects if the prediction was wrong.
entries.whatItIs: UE5's CharacterMovementComponent implements a client-side prediction and server reconciliation system where the owning client predicts movement locally for responsive gameplay, sends timestamped input snapshots (Saved Moves) to the server for validation, and replays pending moves if the server issues a correction.
whenToUse: When building any multiplayer game with player-controlled characters that move
When you need responsive movement without waiting for server round-trip latency
When you want built-in support for walking, falling, swimming, and flying movement modes
When extending with custom movement modes that need prediction support
whenNotToUse: For vehicle movement — use a dedicated vehicle movement component instead
For non-character entities like projectiles or physics objects that use their own replication
When building a fully server-authoritative game where client prediction is not desired (turn-based)
For AI-controlled characters that only need server-side movement (no prediction needed)
commonMistakes: Not overriding FSavedMove_Character when adding custom input data, causing desync between predicted and corrected movement
Applying movement logic only on the client without mirroring it on the server, leading to constant corrections
Forgetting that root motion must be replicated via RootMotionSource for proper prediction
Setting NetworkSmoothingMode incorrectly, causing visible rubber-banding on simulated proxies
Running gameplay logic in the movement prediction path that has side effects (spawning actors, dealing damage) — prediction can replay multiple times
keyPoints: Client predicts movement locally for responsive feel (no waiting for server)
Client sends Saved Moves to server: timestamped input snapshots
Server re-simulates the movement and sends corrections if prediction was wrong
If correction happens, client replays all pending moves from the corrected position
Simulated proxies (other players) use interpolation/extrapolation for smooth display
NetworkSmoothingMode: Disabled, Linear, Exponential — controls how corrections appear
For custom movement modes: override FSavedMove_Character to include custom input data
